493	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
494	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
495	
496	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
497	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
498	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
499	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
500	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
501	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
502	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
503	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
504	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
505	
506	\begin{lemma}\leanok
507	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
508	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
510	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
511	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
512	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
513	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
514	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
515	  \(F\):
516	  \[
517	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
518	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
519	  \]
520	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
521	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
522	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
523	  the Cauchy product.
524	\end{lemma}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}\leanok
527	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
528	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
529	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
530	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
531	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
532	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
533	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
534	  \[
535	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
536	  \]
537	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
538	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
539	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
540	  \[
541	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
542	  \]
543	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
544	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
545	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
546	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	\begin{proof}
550	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
551	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
552	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
553	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
554	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
555	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
556	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
557	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
558	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
559	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
560	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
561	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
562	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
563	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
564	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
565	\end{proof}
566	
567	\begin{definition}\leanok
568	[Rational Hilbert function]
569	  \label{def:ratHilb}
570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
571	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
572	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
573	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
574	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
575	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
576	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
577	  \[
578	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
579	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
580	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
581	  \]
582	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
583	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
584	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
585	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
586	\end{definition}
587	
588	\begin{lemma}\leanok
589	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
590	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
591	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
592	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
593	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
594	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
595	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
596	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
597	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
598	\end{lemma}
599	
600	\begin{lemma}\leanok
601	[Raising the pole order]
602	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
604	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
605	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
606	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
607	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
608	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
609	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
610	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
611	\end{lemma}
612	
613	\begin{lemma}\leanok
614	[Closure under difference]
615	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
617	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
618	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
619	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
620	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
621	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
622	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
623	\end{lemma}
624	
625	\begin{lemma}\leanok
626	[Closure under right shift]
627	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
628	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
629	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
630	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
631	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
632	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
633	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
634	  Lean.
635	\end{lemma}
636	
637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
638	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
639	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
641	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
642	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
643	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
644	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
645	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
646	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
647	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
648	\end{lemma}
649	
650	\begin{proof}
651	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
652	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
653	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
654	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
655	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
656	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
657	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
658	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
659	\end{proof}
660	
661	\begin{lemma}\leanok
662	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
663	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
665	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
666	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
667	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
668	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
669	  \[
670	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
671	  \]
672	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
673	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
674	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
675	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
676	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
677	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
678	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
679	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
680	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
681	\end{lemma}
682	
683	\begin{proof}
684	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
685	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
686	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
687	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
688	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
689	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
690	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
691	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
692	\end{proof}

694	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
695	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
697	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
698	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
699	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
700	  % Polynomials" (read from
701	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
702	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
703	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
704	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
705	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
706	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
707	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
708	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
709	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
710	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
711	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
712	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
713	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
714	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
715	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
716	  \[
717	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
718	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
719	  \]
720	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
721	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
722	\end{theorem}
723	
724	\begin{proof}
725	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
726	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
727	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
728	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
729	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
730	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
731	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
732	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
733	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
734	  function
735	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
736	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
737	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
738	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
739	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
740	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
741	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
742	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
743	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
744	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
745	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
746	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
747	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
748	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
749	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
750	\end{proof}

752	\subsection{The graded-module wrapper for the Stacks~00K1 inductive step}
753	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
754	
755	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
756	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
757	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction: to apply the
758	inductive hypothesis to the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
759	element, those two modules must be presented as finitely generated graded modules
760	over the smaller ring \(R/(x)\). This subsection records the small wrapper that
761	manufactures that presentation. It is a thin layer over existing Mathlib scaffold:
762	Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
763	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the quotient \(M/xM\) as a named
764	object (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of homogeneous
765	elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
766	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
767	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib});
768	what is missing -- and supplied here -- is the \emph{induced grading} on the
769	derived objects (a homogeneous submodule, a homogeneous quotient module, the
770	quotient ring \(R/(x)\)) together with the regrading and finite-module transfer
771	over \(R/(x)\). Each block supplies an internal direct sum decomposition of the
772	relevant module together with the associated graded scalar multiplication.
773	
774	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
775	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
776	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a
777	finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\).
778	Fix a homogeneous element \(x \in R_1\). Write \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\)
779	for the graded kernel of multiplication by \(x\) and \(C = M/xM\) for the cokernel.
780

868	\subsubsection*{Induced gradings on the derived objects}
869	
870	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ -- grading on a homogeneous submodule]
871	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
873	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
874	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(R\)-module and let
875	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
876	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
877	  \(n \mapsto p \cap M_n\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces is an internal direct sum
878	  decomposition of \(p\) -- i.e. \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) internally --
879	  and the \(R\)-action on \(M\) restricts to a graded scalar multiplication on
880	  \(p\). In particular the graded
881	  kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\), which is a homogeneous submodule (an element
882	  \(m = \sum_n m_n\) satisfies \(x m = 0\) iff each \(x m_n = 0\), because the
883	  \(x m_n\) lie in distinct degrees), inherits the grading
884	  \(K_n = K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\).
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
889	  The internal decomposition of \(M\) restricts to \(p\). For \(m \in p\), write
890	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) (the
891	  decomposition of \(M\)); homogeneity of \(p\) gives \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\),
892	  hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p \cap M_n\). This exhibits every element of \(p\)
893	  as a finite sum of elements of the subspaces \(p \cap M_n\), so the canonical map
894	  \(\bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n) \to p\) is surjective; it is injective because the
895	  \(M_n\) are independent in \(M\). Thus the family is an internal direct sum
896	  decomposition of \(p\). For the graded action: if \(a \in R_i\) and
897	  \(m \in p \cap M_n\), then \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by
898	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} and \(a m \in p\) because \(p\)
899	  is a submodule, so \(a m \in p \cap M_{i+n}\), which verifies the graded scalar
900	  multiplication condition. Finally \(K\) is homogeneous: the
901	  components \(x m_n \in M_{n+1}\) of \(x m\) lie in distinct degrees, so
902	  \(x m = 0\) forces each \(x m_n = 0\); thus \(K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to
903	  M_{n+1})\).
904	\end{proof}

906	\begin{lemma}[$G_2$ -- grading on the quotient by a homogeneous submodule]
907	  \label{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}
908	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotient_decomposition}
909	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
910	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
911	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule. Then the quotient module
912	  \(M/p\) has an internal direct sum decomposition with degree-\(n\) component the
913	  image of \(M_n\) under the quotient map \(M \twoheadrightarrow M/p\), together
914	  with a compatible graded scalar multiplication. Specialising to \(p = xM\) gives
915	  the induced grading on \(C = M/xM\) (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), with
916	  components \(C_n = M_n / x M_{n-1}\).
917	\end{lemma}
918	
919	\begin{proof}
920	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
921	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
922	  Surjectivity of the canonical map \(\bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n) \to M/p\) is
923	  immediate: the quotient map is surjective and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\), so every
924	  class \([m]\) is a finite sum of classes \([m_n]\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). For
925	  independence, suppose \(\sum_n [m_n] = 0\) in \(M/p\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). Then
926	  \(\sum_n m_n \in p\); by \(G_1\)
927	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(p =
928	  \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\), so taking degree-\(n\) components gives
929	  \(m_n \in p \cap M_n\), i.e. \([m_n] = 0\) for every \(n\). Hence the components
930	  \(M_n/(p \cap M_n)\) are independent and \(M/p \cong \bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n)\)
931	  is an internal direct sum decomposition. The graded action descends: for
932	  \(a \in R_i\) and a class \([m]\) with \(m \in M_n\), \(a \cdot [m] = [a m]\) and
933	  \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, so the
934	  action lands in component \(i+n\), giving the graded scalar multiplication.
935	  Taking \(p = xM\) -- homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\) by
936	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} -- yields the grading on
937	  \(C = M/xM\).
938	\end{proof}

1029	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1030	
1031	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1032	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1033	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1034	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1035	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1036	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1037	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1038	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1039	  \[
1040	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1041	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1042	  \]
1043	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), \(V = M_n\),
1044	  \(W = M_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi = K_n\)
1045	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) and
1046	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\)
1047	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, since the degree-\((n+1)\) part of
1048	  \(xM\) is \(x M_n\)) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1049	  \[
1050	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
1051	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1052	  \]
1053	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1054	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1055	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1056	\end{lemma}
1057	
1058	\begin{proof}
1059	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1060	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1061	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1062	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1063	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1064	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1065	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1066	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1067	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1068	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1069	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1070	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1071	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1072	  \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
1073	  \(M_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = C_{n+1}\) through the
1074	  gradings of \(G_1\) and \(G_2\), the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded
1075	  piece of the homogeneous submodule \(xM\) is exactly \(x M_n\)
1076	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1077	\end{proof}

1079	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1080	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1081	
1082	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1083	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1084	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1085	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1086	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1087	
1088	\begin{definition}
1089	
1090	\leanok
1091	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1092	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1093	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1094	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1095	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1096	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1097	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1098	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1099	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1100	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1101	  % Polynomials" (read from
1102	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1104	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1105	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1106	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1107	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1109	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1110	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1111	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1112	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1113	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1114	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1115	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1116	  \[
1117	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1118	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1119	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1120	  \]
1121	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1122	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1123	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1124	
1125	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1126	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1127	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1128	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1129	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1130	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1131	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1132	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1133	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1134	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1135	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1136	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1137	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1138	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1139	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1140	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1141	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1142	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1143	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1144	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1145	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1146	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1147	
1148	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1149	\end{definition}

1172	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1173	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1174	  localization]
1175	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1176	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1177	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1178	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1179	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1180	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1181	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1182	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1183	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1184	  \[
1185	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1186	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1187	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1188	  \]
1189	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1190	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1191	
1192	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1193	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1194	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1195	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1196	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1197	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1198	\end{lemma}
1199	
1200	\begin{proof}
1201	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1202	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1203	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1204	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1205	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1206	
1207	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1208	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1209	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1210	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1211	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1212	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1213	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1214	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1215	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1216	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1217	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1218	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1219	\end{proof}

1237	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}

1243	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

1245	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1246	  %  associated to $M$.
1247	  %  [...]

1251	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1252	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1253	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1254	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1255	  \begin{enumerate}
1256	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1257	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1258	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1259	    \item the section-restriction map
1260	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1261	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1262	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1263	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1264	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).

1268	\begin{proof}

1270	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1271	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1272	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1273	
1274	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1275	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1276	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1277	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1278	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1279	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1280	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1281	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1282	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1283	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1284	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1285	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)

1289	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1290	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1291	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.

1473	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
